Kindergarten.topic: Question Set 1: Cardinality and Addition (K.CC.B.4 & K.OA.A.2)
Kindergarten.mastery_q: There are 3 red apples and 2 green apples. How many apples are there in all? Tell me or draw a picture to show how you found the answer.
Kindergarten.mastery_hint: Final Hint: You can count all the apples starting from 1, or start at 3 and count two more.

subs.q: Sub-Question 1: Here is a group of red apples: 🍎 🍎 🍎. Point to each one as you count them. How many are there?
subs.h: Hint: Use your finger to touch each apple as you say the numbers '1, 2...'

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: Here is a group of green apples: 🍏 🍏. How many green apples are there?
subs.h: Hint: Count them just like you did the red ones.

subs.q: Sub-Question 3: If we put the red apples and green apples in one big basket, what is the next number you say after '3' to keep counting?
subs.h: Hint: Start at 3 (the red ones) and count on: '3... 4...'

Kindergarten.mastery_q: Look at the numbers 7 and 3. Tell me which number is greater than the other and explain how you know.
Kindergarten.mastery_hint: Final Hint: Imagine 7 cookies and 3 cookies. Which plate has more?

subs.q: Sub-Question 1: Group A has 4 stars (⭐) and Group B has 6 stars (⭐). How many are in each group?
subs.h: Hint: Count each group carefully and remember the last number you say.

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: If every star in Group A holds hands with a star in Group B, will there be stars left over in Group B?
subs.h: Hint: Draw a line from one star in A to one star in B. See which group has extra.

subs.q: Sub-Question 3: Which number is bigger when we count: 4 or 6?
subs.h: Hint: Think about which number comes later when you count to 10.

Kindergarten.mastery_q: Look at the number 15. Use blocks or a drawing to show me how this number is made of one group of ten and some extra ones. How many extra ones are there?
Kindergarten.mastery_hint: Final Hint: 15 is the same as 10+___.

subs.q: Sub-Question 1: Use these circles: ⚪. Circle a group of exactly 10. How many are left outside your circle? (Provide 13 circles).
subs.h: Hint: Count out ten circles first and draw a big ring around them.

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: If you have 10 circles in a ring and 3 circles outside, how many are there altogether?
subs.h: Hint: Count on from ten: '10... 11, 12, 13.'

subs.q: Sub-Question 3: How do you write the number 'thirteen' using digits?
subs.h: Hint: It has a '1' for the group of ten and a '3' for the extra ones.

Kindergarten.mastery_q: You have 10 apples total. Some are in a red bag and some are in a blue bag. If 4 apples are in the red bag, how many must be in the blue bag? Show your work.
Kindergarten.mastery_hint: Final Hint: Start with 10 fingers or 10 circles. Take away 4. What is left?

subs.q: Sub-Question 1: I have 5 fingers held up. If I tuck 2 fingers down, how many are still up?
subs.h: Hint: Look at your hand and try it!

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: If we have 10 spots on a tens-frame and 8 are filled, how many are empty?
subs.h: Hint: Count the empty boxes in the frame.

subs.q: Sub-Question 3: What number do you need to add to 9 to make 10?
subs.h: Hint: If you have 9, how many more jumps to get to 10?

Kindergarten.topic: Question Set 5: Geometry and Position (K.G.A.1 & K.G.B.4)
Kindergarten.mastery_q: I am a shape. I am solid (3D), I have 6 flat faces that are all squares, and I am sitting beside a cylinder. What shape am I?
Kindergarten.mastery_hint: Final Hint: Think of a toy block or a dice.

subs.q: Sub-Question 1: Look at these shapes. Which one has 3 sides and 3 corners?
subs.h: Hint: It looks like a slice of pie or a mountain.

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: Point to the shape that is above the square. (Provide a diagram).
subs.h: Hint: 'Above' means higher up, like the sun is above the trees.

subs.q: Sub-Question 3: Is a ball a 'flat' shape (2D) or a 'solid' shape (3D)?
subs.h: Hint: Can you hold it in your hand, or is it just a drawing on paper?

Grade 1.topic: Question Set 1: Addition with Three Numbers (1.OA.A.2 & 1.OA.B.3)
Grade 1.mastery_q: Sam has 8 blue blocks, 4 red blocks, and 2 yellow blocks. How many blocks does he have in all?
Grade 1.mastery_hint: Final Hint: Add the 8 and 2 together first to make a ten, then add the 4.

subs.q: Sub-Question 1: What is 8+2?
subs.h: Hint: Use your fingers or a number line to count on from 8.

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: What is 10+4?
subs.h: Hint: Think about one bundle of ten and four extra ones.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 3: Which is the easiest way to solve 8+4+2?
Options: A) 8+4 first, B) 4+2 first, C) 8+2 first, D) Count all on fingers
subs.h: Hint: Look for two numbers that add up to exactly 10.

Grade 1.topic: Question Set 2: Place Value - Tens and Ones (1.NBT.B.2 & 1.NBT.C.4)
Grade 1.mastery_hint: Final Hint: Add one more ten to the tens place, but keep the ones place the same.

subs.q: Sub-Question 1: In the number 43, which digit is in the tens place?
subs.h: Hint: The tens place is the first digit on the left in a 2-digit number.

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: How many ones are in the number 43?
subs.h: Hint: The ones place is the digit on the right.

subs.q: Sub-Question 3: What is 10 more than 40?
subs.h: Hint: Count up by tens: 10, 20, 30, 40... what is next?

Grade 1.mastery_q:
Which math sentence is correct?
Options: A) 42>51, B) 38<35, C) 67>63, D) 21=12
Grade 1.mastery_hint: Final Hint: First look at the tens. If the tens are the same, look at the ones to see which is greater.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 1: Which number has more tens: 52 or 25?
Options: A) 52, B) 25
subs.h: Hint: Look at the first digit of each number.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 2: What does this symbol mean: >?
Options: A) Less than, B) Equal to, C) Greater than
subs.h: Hint: The 'mouth' opens toward the bigger number.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 3: Which number makes this true: 15<___?
Options: A) 10, B) 15, C) 20, D) 5
subs.h: Hint: You need a number that is bigger than 15.

Grade 1.mastery_q: The short hand is between the 7 and the 8. The long hand is on the 6. What time is it?
Grade 1.mastery_hint: Final Hint: It is halfway past the 7. Write the time using a colon (:).

subs.q: Sub-Question 1: On a clock, which hand is shorter: the hour hand or the minute hand?
subs.h: Hint: The shorter hand tells us what hour it is.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 2: When the long minute hand points to the 12, it is:
Options: A) Half past, B) O'clock
subs.h: Hint: This is the start of a brand new hour.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 3: When the long minute hand points to the 6, it is:
Options: A) Half past, B) O'clock
subs.h: Hint: The hand has moved halfway around the circle.

Grade 1.topic: Question Set 5: Geometry - Partitioning Circles and Rectangles (1.G.A.3)
Grade 1.mastery_q:
If you take a square and fold it in half, and then fold it in half again, what are the 4 new equal shapes called?
Options: A) Halves, B) Wholes, C) Fourths, D) Rectangles
Grade 1.mastery_hint: Final Hint: 'Fourths' and 'Quarters' mean the shape is split into 4 equal pieces.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 1: If I cut a cake into 2 parts, but one part is much bigger than the other, are they 'equal shares'?
Options: A) Yes, B) No
subs.h: Hint: Equal shares must be the exact same size.

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: If you divide a circle into two equal shares, what is one of those shares called?
subs.h: Hint: Think of sharing a cookie between two people.

subs.q: Sub-Question 3: How many equal shares are in a 'quarter' of a shape?
subs.h: Hint: It is the same as the number of wheels on a car.

Grade 2.mastery_q: I am a number. I have 5 hundreds, 16 tens, and 2 ones. What number am I in standard form?
Grade 2.mastery_hint: Final Hint: First, find the value of 5 hundreds (500). Then find the value of 16 tens (160). Add 500 + 160 + 2.

subs.q: Sub-Question 1: In the number 706, which digit is in the tens place?
subs.h: Hint: Look at the middle digit. If there are 'no tens', what number represents that?

subs.q:
Sub-Question 2: What is the value of the digit 8 in the number 853?
Options: A) 8, B) 80, C) 800, D) 100
subs.h: Hint: The 8 is in the hundreds place. Think about 8 bundles of 100.

subs.q: Sub-Question 3: Write the number 432 in expanded form (using + signs).
subs.h: Hint: Break it into hundreds + tens + ones (400+___+___).

Grade 2.mastery_q: A baker made 80 muffins. He sold 25 in the morning and 30 in the afternoon. How many muffins does he have left?
Grade 2.mastery_hint: Final Hint: This is a two-step problem. First, add the muffins sold (25+30). Then, subtract that total from 80.

subs.q: Sub-Question 1: There were 35 birds on a tree. 12 more birds flew to the tree. How many are there now?
subs.h: Hint: Use addition to find the new total.

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: There are 47 birds on a tree. 20 birds fly away. How many birds are left?
subs.h: Hint: Use subtraction to find the difference.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 3: If a problem says 'How many more are needed?' which operation should you usually use to find the answer?
Options: A) Addition, B) Subtraction
subs.h: Hint: You are finding the gap between a small number and a target number.

Grade 2.topic: Question Set 3: Measuring and Comparing Lengths (2.MD.A.1 & 2.MD.A.4)
Grade 2.mastery_q: A desk is 45 inches long. A bookshelf is 2 feet long. How many inches longer is the desk? (Note: 1 foot = 12 inches).
Grade 2.mastery_hint: Final Hint: First, turn the 2 feet into inches (12+12=24). Then, subtract 24 from 45.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 1: Which tool is best for measuring the length of a real school bus?
Options: A) A 12-inch ruler, B) A measuring tape, C) A scale, D) A clock
subs.h: Hint: A bus is very long; you need a tool that can reach many feet.

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: Line A is 15 inches long. Line B is 9 inches long. How much longer is Line A than Line B?
subs.h: Hint: Subtract the length of the shorter line from the longer line (15−9).

subs.q: Sub-Question 3: If you place two 10cm pencils end-to-end, what is their total length?
subs.h: Hint: Add 10+10.

Grade 2.mastery_q: An eraser costs 55¢. You have 2 quarters and 2 nickels. Do you have enough money? (Answer 'Yes' or 'No').
Grade 2.mastery_hint: Final Hint: First, find your total money (25+25+5+5). Then compare that number to 55¢.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 1: How many cents is one quarter worth?
Options: A) 5¢, B) 10¢, C) 25¢, D) 50¢
subs.h: Hint: It is the largest common silver coin; four of them make a dollar.

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: You have 2 quarters and 1 dime. How many cents do you have in total?
Sub-Question 3: If the minute hand is pointing at the 8, how many minutes past the hour is it?
subs.h: Hint: Count by fives around the clock: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35...

Grade 2.mastery_q: Partition a rectangle into 4 rows and 3 columns of same-size squares. How many squares are there in total? Write the repeated addition sentence used to find the answer.
Grade 2.mastery_hint: Final Hint: 3+3+3+3 (adding the columns) or 4+4+4 (adding the rows).

subs.q: Sub-Question 1: In an array, do rows go across (left-to-right) or up-and-down?
subs.h: Hint: Think about the rows of seats in a movie theater.

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: A chocolate bar has 3 rows and 4 columns of squares. How many total squares are there?
subs.h: Hint: You can count them all, or add 4+4+4.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 3: Which addition sentence matches an array with 2 rows and 5 columns?
Options: A) 2+5, B) 5+5, C) 2+2+2+2+2, D) Both B and C
subs.h: Hint: You can add the number in each row (5+5) or the number in each column (2+2+2+2+2).

Reception.topic: Question Set 1: Subitising and Cardinality (ELG: Number)
Reception.mastery_q: A plate has a group of 4 crackers. Without counting them individually, you "see" the group. If you eat one cracker, how many are left?
Reception.mastery_hint: Final Hint: Think of the group of 4, then go back one number.

subs.q: Sub-Question 1: Look at a picture of 3 dots arranged like a dice. Without counting them one by one, how many dots are there?
subs.h: Hint: Try to "see" the group all at once without using your finger to point.

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: If you have a row of 5 buttons and you count them "1, 2, 3, 4, 5," what is the total number of buttons?
subs.h: Hint: The last number you say is the answer.

subs.q: Sub-Question 3: You have 4 apples. If you add one more apple to the group, how many do you have now?
subs.h: Hint: What is the next number after 4?

Reception.topic: Question Set 2: Number Composition to 5 (ELG: Number)
Reception.mastery_q: Five birds are on a fence. Some are big and some are small. If there is at least one of each, and 2 are big, how many are small?
Reception.mastery_hint: Final Hint: You are looking for the "partner" number to 2 that completes the 5.

subs.q: Sub-Question 1: If 3 red cars and 2 blue cars are in a garage, how many cars are there in total?
subs.h: Hint: Combine the 3 and the 2.

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: I have 5 stickers. If 4 are on my hand, how many are still on the page?
subs.h: Hint: 4 and what other number make 5?

subs.q: Sub-Question 3: What is 5 take away 0?
subs.h: Hint: If you have 5 and take nothing away, the number stays the same.

Reception.topic: Question Set 3: Comparing Quantities (ELG: Numerical Patterns)
Reception.mastery_q:
There are 3 plates of cookies. Plate X has 7, Plate Y has 5, and Plate Z has 7. Which two plates are equal?
Options: A) X and Y B) Y and Z C) X and Z
Reception.mastery_hint: Final Hint: Look for the two plates that have the exact same number.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 1: Group A has 8 blocks. Group B has 3 blocks. Which group has more?
Options: A) Group A B) Group B
subs.h: Hint: Which number is bigger when we count?

subs.q:
Sub-Question 2: Group C has 5 blocks. Group D has 9 blocks. Which group has fewer?
Options: A) Group C B) Group D
subs.h: Hint: Which number comes first when we count?

subs.q: Sub-Question 3: If I have 6 sweets and you have 6 sweets, do we have a different amount or the same amount?
subs.h: Hint: If the numbers are the same, the amount is "equal."

Reception.topic: Question Set 4: Even/Odd and Distribution (ELG: Numerical Patterns)
Reception.mastery_q: You have 10 socks. If you put them into pairs (groups of 2), how many pairs will you have?
Reception.mastery_hint: Final Hint: How many times can you count 2 until you reach 10?

subs.q: Sub-Question 1: If every child in a group of 4 needs a pair of shoes, how many shoes are there in total?
subs.h: Hint: Count by twos: 2, 4, 6... or double the number 4.

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: If you share 6 cookies equally between 2 friends, how many cookies does each friend get?
subs.h: Hint: One for you, one for me. Split the 6 in half.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 3: Can you share 5 apples equally between 2 people without breaking an apple?
Options: A) Yes B) No
subs.h: Hint: If there is one left over, it is an "odd" number.

Reception.topic: Question Set 5: Counting Beyond 20 (ELG: Numerical Patterns)
Reception.mastery_q: If you are counting blocks and you just said the number "twenty-nine," what is the next number you will say?
Reception.mastery_hint: Final Hint: After the 20s come the 30s.

subs.q: Sub-Question 1: What number comes right after 19?
subs.h: Hint: Count up from 15: 15, 16, 17, 18, 19...

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: When counting, what number comes right after 20?
subs.h: Hint: It starts with "twenty-" and ends with "one."

subs.q:
Sub-Question 3: Which number is missing in this pattern: 20, 21, 22, __, 24?
Options: A) 25 B) 23 C) 30
subs.h: Hint: What is one more than 22?

Year 1.topic: Question Set 1: Number and Place Value (1 More/1 Less)
Year 1.mastery_q: I am a number. If you find 1 more than me, you get 20. What number am I?
Year 1.mastery_hint: Final Hint: To find the starting number, you need to go backwards 1 step from 20.

subs.q: Sub-Question 1: What is the next number in this counting pattern? 12, 13, 14, 15, ___
subs.h: Hint: Think about the number that comes right after 15 when you count.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 2: Which number is 1 more than 14?
Options: A) 13, B) 15, C) 14, D) 24
subs.h: Hint: If you add one more block to a pile of 14, how many will you have?

subs.q:
Sub-Question 3: Which number is 1 less than 19?
Options: A) 20, B) 18, C) 17, D) 10
subs.h: Hint: Think of the number that comes right before 19.

Year 1.topic: Question Set 2: Addition and Subtraction (Number Bonds within 20)
Year 1.mastery_hint: Final Hint: Look at the = sign. It means the left side (15) must be the same as the right side. What do you add to 10 to get 15?

subs.q:
Sub-Question 1: Which sign means "add together"?
Options: A) –, B) =, C) +, D) x
subs.h: Hint: It is the sign that looks like a small cross.

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: Fill in the missing number: 7 + ___ = 10
subs.h: Hint: If you have 7 apples, how many more do you need to make 10?

subs.q: Sub-Question 3: What is 9 - 4?
subs.h: Hint: Start at 9 and count back 4 steps.

Year 1.mastery_q: Tom has 8 stickers. He gives half to Ben. Then Ben gives half of his stickers to Sam. How many stickers does Sam have?
Year 1.mastery_hint: Final Hint: First, find half of 8. Then, take that answer and find half of it again.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 1: If a pizza is cut into 2 equal parts, what is each part called?
Options: A) A quarter, B) A whole, C) A half, D) A third
subs.h: Hint: Think about sharing a sandwich exactly between 2 people.

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: There are 6 sweets. If you give half to a friend, how many sweets do they get?
subs.h: Hint: Split the 6 sweets into two groups that are exactly the same size.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 3: If a square is cut into 4 equal parts, each part is a:
Options: A) Half, B) Quarter, C) Whole, D) Circle
subs.h: Hint: "Quarter" sounds like the number 4.

Year 1.topic: Question Set 4: Measurement (Time - O'clock and Half Past)
Year 1.mastery_q: School starts at 9 o'clock. Lunch is half an hour after 12 o'clock. Write the time for lunch.
Year 1.mastery_hint: Final Hint: Combine the hour (12) with the name for when the long hand is at the 6.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 1: On a clock, the long hand points to 12. What part of the time does this show?
Options: A) Half past, B) O'clock, C) Minutes, D) Seconds
subs.h: Hint: When the long hand is at the very top, we say "__ o'clock."

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: The short hand is on 3 and the long hand is on 12. What time is it?
subs.h: Hint: Look at the short hand to see the hour.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 3: When the long hand points to the 6, we call this time:
Options: A) 6 o'clock, B) Half past, C) 12 o'clock, D) Morning
subs.h: Hint: The hand has traveled halfway around the clock.

Year 1.mastery_q:
I have 4 straight sides. My sides are all the same length. Which shape am I?
Options: A) Triangle, B) Square, C) Circle, D) Rectangle
Year 1.mastery_hint: Final Hint: A rectangle has 4 sides, but only a _____ has sides that are all equal.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 1: Which shape has 3 sides and 3 corners?
Options: A) Square, B) Circle, C) Triangle, D) Rectangle
subs.h: Hint: Think of the shape of a slice of pizza.

subs.q: Sub-Question 2: How many sides does a Rectangle have?
subs.h: Hint: Count the straight lines that make the shape.

subs.q:
Sub-Question 3: Which of these shapes has no straight sides and no corners?
Options: A) Square, B) Triangle, C) Circle, D) Rectangle
subs.h: Hint: This shape is perfectly round.
